input.subtitle: Site Walk-Through Conversation Guide — Oil & Grease Pretreatment

subStreams: Industrial discharge / categorical pretreatment compliance — oil & grease
header.stageIntro: Deal is at Scope. Customer has shared pretreatment layout and agreed to a site walk-through. Regional Ops VP is confirmed engaged at >$150K. This conversation must close the three Required gaps: loading data, competitive landscape, and budget/procurement vehicle.
header.insight: The plant manager sharing their pretreatment drawings signals solution-orientation, not just violation management. Use the site walk to pin the loading numbers, surface what alternatives they're weighing, and earn the framing the Ops VP will hear.

themes.title: Open on the operational reality
themes.framing: Start by walking the existing pretreatment train with the plant manager. Get them describing the system in their own words before you say anything about solutions.
questions: Walk me through your current pretreatment setup — what's running, in what order, and what was it originally designed to handle?
Where in the process do you think the oil and grease is coming from — wash chemistry, rinse carryover, something upstream?
How often are you pulling samples today, and who's doing the sampling — your team or the POTW?
whyItMatters: Loading data (concentrations + flow) is the single gap that widens the deal size range most. Get them talking about it operationally — the numbers will follow.

themes.title: Pin the regulatory exposure
themes.framing: Understand the exact enforcement state — how many NOVs, what the POTW's posture is, and whether a consent order or compliance schedule is already in play.
questions: How many violation notices have you received, and how are they framed — informal warning, formal NOV, something else?
Has the POTW said anything about a compliance schedule or consent order, or are you still in the informal-response window?
Are they charging you surcharges on top of the standard discharge rate today, or does the violation just trigger the NOV letter?
What's your sense of how much runway you have before this escalates — do you have a re-inspection date or a response deadline?
whyItMatters: Surcharge history and enforcement timeline are the two inputs that anchor the cost-of-alternative table. If the POTW is already billing surcharges, that's recurring cash bleed we can quantify.

themes.title: Surface the cost they're already carrying
themes.framing: This is the cost-of-alternative conversation in disguise. You're not pitching yet — you're helping them account for what doing nothing (or the cheap path) is actually costing.
questions: What's the POTW charging you for the O&G overages — surcharge per unit, flat penalty, reinspection fees?
What have you spent internally managing this so far — operator time, lab costs, any temporary fixes or chemical trials?
If the situation stays where it is for another year, what does that look like financially and operationally for the plant?
Has anyone put a number on what a consent order compliance timeline might cost you — legal, engineering, reporting?

themes.title: Understand the current pretreatment config in detail
themes.framing: Walk the physical system. You need the treatment-train layout to size the solution. The plant manager shared drawings — verify against what's actually installed.
questions: Is what you sent me the current as-built, or has anything been modified since those drawings?
What's your approximate flow rate through the pretreatment system — average day vs. peak wash cycle?
What's the condition of the existing equipment — is it maintaining its design performance, or are you seeing degradation?
Is there any segregation between wash water and rinse water streams, or does everything combine before pretreatment?
whyItMatters: Flow rate, treatment train condition, and stream segregation are the three variables that pin whether we're talking about an upgrade to the existing system or a parallel/replacement installation.

themes.title: Surface what alternatives they're weighing
themes.framing: You need to know what else is in the conversation — other vendors, an internal fix, or doing nothing. Don't assume you're the only call they've made.
questions: Have you talked to anyone else about addressing this — other equipment suppliers, your POTW's preferred vendor list, your corporate engineering team?
Has anyone proposed a solution yet, even informally — and if so, what did it look like?
Is there an internal option being considered — a DIY upgrade, a process change, switching detergent chemistry?
What would make you feel confident that whatever you do actually solves it — are you looking for a guaranteed compliance outcome or the lowest-cost first step?
whyItMatters: If another vendor is already proposing, this deal is closer to Position than Scope and the competitive framing needs to shift now.

themes.title: Anchor the budget and procurement path
themes.framing: You know the Ops VP threshold is $150K. Get clarity on how capital decisions work at this plant — cycle timing, approval process, and whether there are any funding mechanisms in play.
questions: How does a capital project like this typically get funded at your facilities — is this a plant-level capex decision or does it go through a corporate capital cycle?
Is there a budget cycle coming up where this would need to be approved — do you have a window in mind?
Would you approach this as a traditional capex purchase, or is there any appetite for a financed or service-based structure where the upfront is lower?
If this comes in above $150K, is the regional ops VP already briefed enough to move quickly, or would they need a full presentation?

themes.title: Understand the operations and maintenance reality
themes.framing: The right solution has to fit the team running it. Understand operator capacity and skill level — it shapes both the solution design and the ongoing service scope.
questions: Who runs the pretreatment system day-to-day — a dedicated operator, a maintenance tech, or does it rotate?
How much time are they spending on it right now, and what's their background — water treatment experience or more general maintenance?
If we add or upgrade the system, what level of ongoing support would you want from us versus handling it internally?
Have you had issues in the past with pretreatment systems that looked good on paper but were hard to operate consistently?

themes.title: Surface the phase-2 horizon
themes.framing: Oil and grease is the presenting problem, but industrial laundry operations have emerging exposure on other parameters. Surface awareness without creating noise — this is a credibility builder, not a scope expansion pitch.
questions: Have you had any other parameters come up on your discharge reports — pH swings, surfactants, anything else the POTW has flagged?
Are you tracking any changes in your wash chemistry or product mix that might affect your effluent profile going forward?
Has your POTW said anything about tightening their local limits or changing their pretreatment program requirements in the next few years?
whyItMatters: An O&G fix that leaves the customer exposed on surfactants or pH in 18 months is a short-term win and a long-term relationship problem. Surface it now.

themes.title: Frame what success looks like for the Ops VP
themes.framing: You need to understand the Ops VP's success frame before you propose — because the proposal will need to clear their review. The plant manager can tell you how to land it.
questions: When you bring this to the regional ops VP, what's the story you want to be able to tell — compliance fix, cost savings, operational reliability?
Is there a format or level of detail they'd expect — a one-pager, a full engineering proposal, references from other sites?
What's the biggest concern they're likely to raise — upfront cost, disruption to operations, confidence it'll actually work?
Would it be useful to have us present directly to the ops VP, or is it better if you bring the recommendation internally?

themes.title: Test the value frame
themes.framing: Before you close the site visit, test which win-win anchor lands hardest — compliance certainty, cost of the alternative, or operational simplicity. This tells you how to lead the proposal.
questions: If I could show you that fixing this definitively costs less over five years than the ongoing surcharges plus the enforcement exposure, would that be the right frame for the ops VP?
Or is the bigger concern operational — making sure this doesn't create a new headache for the plant team?
What would make this an easy yes for you internally — a performance guarantee, a phased approach, something else?

themes.title: Lock the next step
themes.framing: Leave the site visit with a committed next step, a timeline, and clarity on whether the Ops VP needs to be in the room before you propose.
questions: Based on what we've seen today, would it make sense for us to come back with a scoped proposal — or is there another step you'd want first?
What's a realistic timeline for you — when would you want to have a solution in place, and does that work back to a decision point we should hit?
Is there anything you'd want from us before you take this to the ops VP — a one-page summary, a rough cost range, something else?
Can we schedule a follow-up call with the ops VP included, or does that happen after you've reviewed what we put together?

finalText:
---

**Stage: Scope — MEDIUM confidence.** Here's the read and your Playbook:

---

### Stage call

This deal is at **Scope**. The advance signals are clean: the plant manager has shared pretreatment drawings (operational engagement on the work itself), two follow-up calls are in, and — most importantly — the site walk-through has been agreed. That last step is the textbook Qualify→Scope transition event. MEDIUM confidence rather than HIGH because we don't yet have loading data, competitive landscape clarity, or budget/procurement vehicle confirmed.

**What advances you to Position** is exactly what the site walk-through should close:
1. Actual O&G concentrations + flow rate (pins deal sizing)
2. Whether other vendors or an internal fix are in the conversation
3. Budget envelope and procurement vehicle (capex cycle, financed, EaaS?)
4. A direct line to the Ops VP before you propose — not after

The $150K threshold being volunteered by the plant manager is a meaningful tell: they already have a deal size in mind that clears the VP approval bar. That's not a red flag — it's positioning signal. Use it.

---

### Site Walk-Through Playbook

📄 **[Download Playbook PDF](?threadId=eval-edge-case-mixed-stage-and-playbook-ask-Tt53T1XCvqkPWLCW6ZaSA&kind=playbook)**

The Playbook is organised around 11 themes calibrated for this Scope-stage site visit. The three must-hit questions on the day:

1. **"What is the POTW charging you for the O&G overages — surcharges, reinspection fees, any consent order commitments?"** — this is your cost-of-alternative anchor; don't leave the site without it.
2. **"Have you talked to anyone else about addressing this — another vendor, your corporate engineering team, an internal fix?"** — if another vendor is already proposing, you're at Position and the competitive framing needs to shift now.
3. **"When you bring this to the regional ops VP, what's the story you want to tell — compliance fix, cost savings, operational reliability?"** — earns VP-level framing before you propose, not after.

The Playbook also covers: walking the existing pretreatment config in detail, surfacing the phase-2 horizon (surfactants, pH, wash chemistry changes), anchoring the budget and procurement path, and locking a committed next step before you leave the site.
